title: Ink on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-lane immediately; do not improvise at home
stainChemistry: Ink — ballpoint, gel, or permanent marker — is concentrated dye in a solvent base. Each ink type needs a different solvent, and the solvents that lift ink from cotton damage silk on contact. Rubbing alcohol strips silk dye where it lands, water alone leaves permanent rings on silk, and acetone destroys the fiber. The household ink-removal toolkit causes three different kinds of damage on silk. Pro is the only safe path on ink plus silk.
whyThisWorks: A pro spotter selects the right controlled solvent for the ink type, applies it with tight blotting and a clean rinse, and never gives the silk time to ring. Home protocol is to blot any wet ink fast, leave the rest alone, and get to a silk specialist immediately. Most household ink-removal tools damage silk worse than the ink does.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): ballpoint ink is dye (Crystal Violet, eosin) in oil-based vehicle plus resin binder; gel pen is pigment particles in water-glycol; permanent marker is solvent dye in xylene/toluene plus resin. Pro uses VDS or amyl acetate for the resin/solvent phase plus sodium hydrosulfite at controlled dilution for residual chromophore — chemistry that lifts ink without dye-stripping silk fibroin.
defaultAssumption: Silk + ink = pro IMMEDIATELY. Any home solvent attempt creates a worse problem than the ink. Treat as professional-only from minute one.

spottingProtocol.agent: Identify Ink Type + Catch Cloth Under
spottingProtocol.equipment: Good light, clean white cotton cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Identify: ballpoint (oil + dye), gel (water + pigment), or permanent marker (solvent + dye + resin). Each takes a different pro solvent. Slip a clean white catch cloth UNDER the silk where possible — dissolved ink transfers DOWN.

spottingProtocol.equipment: VDS or amyl acetate per ink type, white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pro selects solvent by ink type. Apply to cloth, tamp tight concentric passes from edge inward. Flip cloth frequently. Do NOT flood silk.
Cold NSD removes residual pigment + solvent. Press only, no agitation.
For residual dye color, reducing bleach is silk-safer than oxidizer. Strict 60-sec ceiling.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold Water Final Rinse + Press-Dry Flat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush until clear. Press between dry towels, lay flat to air-dry.

steps: 2. Catch cloth under + tight tamp blotting from edge in.
3. VDS or amyl acetate per ink type, controlled dwell.
5. Sodium hydrosulfite for residual color, 60-sec ceiling.
6. Cold final rinse, press dry, lay flat.
warnings: NEVER isopropyl alcohol on silk — strips silk dyes (cotton tool, silk poison)
NEVER acetone on silk — damages silk protein + dye bleed
NEVER flood silk with water — permanent rings
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes
Solvent selection by ink type — the wrong solvent does nothing OR damages silk

homeSolutions.instruction: Silk + ink is the rare combination where every common home tool causes new damage. Rubbing alcohol (the cotton ink-removal standard) STRIPS SILK DYES — leaves a paler patch wherever you apply it. Water alone leaves PERMANENT RINGS on silk. Acetone damages the silk protein AND can bleed silk dye. The cotton playbook doesn't transfer. Home protocol is exactly two things: blot any wet ink, then get to a silk specialist immediately. Do not try anything else.

homeSolutions.agent: If Wet — Blot Once, Outside-In, Then Stop
homeSolutions.instruction: If the ink is still wet (just dropped), press a clean white cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the pool — outside edges first, working toward the center. Lift straight up, press a fresh part of the cloth, repeat 2–3 light dabs. Lift bulk wet ink only. Do NOT rub, smear, or wipe — wiping spreads ink across more silk. Do NOT add water — water rings silk.

homeSolutions.agent: If Dry — Leave It Completely Alone
homeSolutions.instruction: Dried ink on silk is BETTER for a pro to handle than disturbed ink. Do NOT pick at it with a fingernail. Do NOT scrape — scraping tears silk protein. Do NOT attempt to soften with anything. Silk is fragile; leave the ink in place until the cleaner can apply controlled solvent.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro Silk Specialist IMMEDIATELY — Identify Ink Type
homeSolutions.instruction: Call ahead: "Do you treat ink on silk with VDS or amyl acetate?" If they don't know what those are, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them BOTH: (1) the ink type — ballpoint, gel pen, or permanent marker (each takes a different solvent), and (2) "NOTHING applied at home. No alcohol, no water, no acetone, no scrubbing." That handoff is valuable — the pro can go straight to the right solvent at the right concentration.

steps: 1. Silk + ink = pro IMMEDIATELY. Do not improvise.
2. If wet: 2–3 light dabs straight down with white cloth. Stop.
3. If dry: leave alone. Do not pick, scrape, or apply anything.
4. Pro silk specialist with ink type identified: ballpoint / gel / Sharpie.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after the initial dab on wet ink. Any solvent or water at home creates new damage worse than the ink.
warnings: NEVER alcohol on silk — strips silk dyes
NEVER water on silk for ink — permanent rings
NEVER acetone — damages silk protein + dye bleed
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes
NEVER scraping dried ink — tears silk protein
Wrong solvent selection = no progress OR new damage

neverDo: Never IPA / rubbing alcohol on silk
Never scrape dried ink — tears silk
Never improvise solvent selection at home
fiberSensitivities: Silk silk protein damaged by acetone + most aggressive solvents
Silk dyes stripped by isopropyl alcohol
Silk rings permanently from water alone

materialWarnings: The cotton ink-removal playbook DAMAGES silk
Alcohol strips silk dyes — pale patch where you apply it
Water rings silk — as visible as ink

escalation.when: Always — silk + ink is pro-only IMMEDIATELY. Even minor home attempts create new damage.
escalation.whatToTell: "Ink on silk — type [ballpoint / gel pen / permanent marker], age [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No alcohol, no water, no acetone, no scrubbing."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm VDS / amyl acetate spotting + ink type chemistry)

contextQuestions: Ink type — ballpoint (oil/dye), gel pen (water/pigment), or permanent marker (solvent/dye/resin)?
Type of silk — charmeuse / chiffon / habotai / dupioni?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Ink type drives solvent selection — pro can't help without knowing. Pre-applied IPA = silk dye likely already stripped. Pre-applied water = ring already present. Disclose honestly. Permanent marker (Sharpie) is the hardest variant on silk.
